modules.title: Module 1: Understanding Fire
modules.description: The fundamentals of fire behaviour in care settings

lessons.title: The Fire Triangle
lessons.body: Fire requires three elements to ignite and sustain itself: Heat, Fuel, and Oxygen — together forming the Fire Triangle. In a care home environment, all three elements are commonly present. Understanding this triangle is the foundation of all fire prevention work. Remove any one element and the fire cannot continue.
key_points: Remove one element to extinguish fire

lessons.title: Common Causes of Fire in Care Homes
lessons.body: The most frequent causes of fire in care homes include electrical faults, kitchen appliances left unattended, smoking materials disposed of incorrectly, and overloaded electrical sockets. Care home staff must identify and report potential fire hazards as part of their daily duties.
key_points: Kitchen hazards — never leave cooking unattended
lessons.regulatory_reference: CQC Safe Care and Treatment Regulation 12

lessons.title: Fire Risk in Care Environments
lessons.body: Care homes present unique fire risks because many residents have limited mobility or cognitive impairments making evacuation difficult. A Personal Emergency Evacuation Plan (PEEP) must exist for every resident and must be reviewed regularly.
key_points: Staff must know every resident's PEEP
lessons.regulatory_reference: The Care Act 2014 / CQC Fundamental Standards

modules.title: Module 2: Prevention and Response
modules.description: How to prevent fires and respond correctly when they occur

lessons.title: Fire Prevention Duties
lessons.body: Every member of care staff has a legal duty to take reasonable steps to prevent fire. This includes conducting daily visual checks, ensuring fire doors are never propped open, reporting faulty equipment immediately, and keeping escape routes clear at all times.
key_points: Fire doors must never be propped open

lessons.title: Raising the Alarm
lessons.body: If you discover a fire or see smoke, your immediate action is to raise the alarm without delay. Activate the nearest fire alarm call point. Do not attempt to fight the fire first. Do not collect personal belongings. Begin evacuation immediately.
lessons.regulatory_reference: BS 5839-1:2017 Fire Detection and Alarm Systems

lessons.title: Evacuation Procedures
lessons.body: All care staff must know the Evacuation Assembly Points, all fire exit locations, and how to assist residents to evacuate safely. The procedure is: Alert — Evacuate — Close doors — Assemble. Never use lifts during a fire. Complete roll call at the assembly point.
key_points: Know all fire exits and assembly points
Complete roll call at assembly point

questions.question: What are the three elements of the Fire Triangle?
questions.explanation: The Fire Triangle consists of Heat, Fuel, and Oxygen. Removing any one element extinguishes the fire.

questions.question: What is your FIRST action if you discover a fire?
questions.explanation: Raising the alarm immediately is always the first action. Every second counts.

questions.question: What does PEEP stand for?
questions.explanation: A PEEP is required for every resident who may need assistance evacuating.

questions.question: Fire doors in a care home should be:
questions.explanation: Fire doors must be kept closed at all times to contain fire and smoke.

questions.question: Which law places a legal duty on staff to prevent fire?
questions.explanation: The Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 places legal duties on all responsible persons.
